title: What to do with fermented bokashi waste
subtitle: The second stage after the bokashi bucket
excerpt: The output of a bokashi bucket is pickled, not composted. It is acidic and not directly usable as compost. The second stage either buries it in soil to be neutralised by soil microbes, adds it to a hot compost heap, or processes it in a soil factory.
sourceNotes: Bokashi composting guidance from EM (Effective Microorganisms) UK research. Garden Organic bokashi guidance. Journal of Applied Microbiology: soil treatment of bokashi ferment pH normalisation.
aliases: what to do with bokashi output

glossaryTerms.definition: An anaerobic fermentation method using an inoculated bran (EM bran) to acidify and preserve food waste including meat and dairy. The fermented output has a pH of approximately 3.5-4.5. It is not compost and cannot be used directly on plants.

The liquid that drains from a bokashi bucket during fermentation. Bokashi leachate is highly acidic (pH 3-4) and must be diluted before use. Diluted at 100:1 with water, it can be poured down drains as a mild drain cleaner; diluted at 200:1, it may be used as a soil drench.

Bokashi ferment is high in nitrogen (food scraps, including proteins). The acidic pH inhibits bacterial activity until neutralised. Once buried in soil or added to a compost heap, the nitrogen becomes available quickly after neutralisation.

content.text: bucket contains fermented, preserved food waste at pH 3.5 to 4.5: acidic enough to suppress further decomposition but not yet converted to compost. Applied directly to soil or plants at this pH, it will burn roots. The second stage neutralises the acidity.

Dig a trench 20 to 30 cm deep. Add the bokashi ferment in a layer no thicker than 10 cm and cover immediately with the excavated soil. Do not plant in that location for 2 to 4 weeks while the soil microbes neutralise the acidity. The
in the ferment becomes plant-available quickly once pH normalises, typically within 2 to 4 weeks in summer, longer in winter. Mark the buried location and plant after the waiting period.
In winter when ground is frozen or fully occupied with growing plants, store the full bucket in a cool place (it keeps for up to 4 weeks sealed) until a burial spot is available.
Option 2: addition to a hot compost heap

A hot compost heap actively decomposing at 55 to 65°C will neutralise bokashi ferment rapidly. Bury the ferment into the centre of the heap and cover with a 30 cm layer of existing compost material. Do not add to a cold or inactive heap: the acidity will inhibit the existing microbial activity. Do not add to a wormery: the low pH and fermentation products will harm the worms.

A soil factory is a large container or bin filled with a mix of soil and mature compost (approximately equal parts). Bokashi ferment is added in layers and mixed in. The large soil volume buffers the acidity and the existing microbial population neutralises it rapidly. After 2 to 4 weeks, the resulting material is a nutrient-rich soil amendment. A soil factory is the best approach for households without a hot compost heap or easily accessible garden soil.

from the bucket sump is highly acidic (pH 3 to 4) and must not be applied undiluted to soil or drains. It can be poured down kitchen drains diluted at 100:1 with cold water; the acidity acts as a mild cleaner for biofilm. Do not use on septic tanks as the effective microorganisms may disrupt the septic system biology.

items.symptom: Ferment smells strongly of ammonia or decay rather than pickled/vinegary
items.cause: Bucket was not fully anaerobic; rotting has occurred rather than fermentation
items.fix: This output should not be used in a soil factory or near plants. Dispose via general waste. Ensure the lid is sealed airtight at every addition in future.

items.symptom: Nothing seems to be happening in the burial site
items.cause: Winter soil temperatures slow neutralisation significantly
items.fix: Wait longer: 6-8 weeks in winter rather than 2-4 weeks in summer. Check with a pH meter or litmus paper at the burial depth if uncertain.
